title: Hyppönen — *If It's Smart, It's Vulnerable* AI chapter (Deep Blue / Kasparov / 'unnatural intelligence')
html:
Page from Mikko Hyppönen's If It's Smart, It's Vulnerable — the AI chapter opener. Hyppönen recounts reading about AI in 1984 in Tekniikan Maailma (the Finnish Popular Science), where superhuman computers were called "unnatural intelligence." The benchmark was chess; the magazine predicted a Grandmaster-beating computer would mean AI "is here." 13 years later in May 1997, IBM's Deep Blue beat Kasparov — but by then attitudes had shifted: Deep Blue was reframed as "just brute calculation." The chapter's frame: humans keep moving the goalposts after every milestone is met.

TL;DR

  • Hyppönen frames the AI debate around the moving-goalposts problem: every benchmark we set (chess, Go, Turing test, etc.) gets dismissed as "not real intelligence" once a machine clears it.
  • The Deep Blue → Kasparov 1997 milestone is his anchor for why "AGI is here" arguments have always been retrofitted away by skeptics.
  • He predicts genuine general AI before long — "the second most intelligent creatures on the planet" framing.

mikko hypponen · if its smart its vulnerable · chatgpt · karpathy
